Budget.title: Budget
Budget.description: Represents a sales budget within the Florencio Sales Manager application.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Budget entity.

name.description: Name of the budget, providing a descriptive label.

status.description: Current status of the budget (New, Pending, Approved, Loss, Rejected).

value.description: The monetary value associated with the budget.

creationDate.description: Date and time when the budget was initially created.

rejectionReason.description: The stated reason for the budget's rejection, if applicable.

followUpSuggested.description: Suggested content for a follow-up message (generated by AI).

definition.description: Stores all budget documents.  Security rules will ensure only authenticated users can read or write (business logic performed in a trusted environment).

params.name: budgetId
params.description: The unique identifier of the budget.

firestore.reasoning:
Based on the provided application requirements for the Florencio Sales Manager, the Firestore data structure is designed with a focus on simplicity, security, and scalability, adhering to the principles of Authorization Independence and Structural Segregation. Since all budgets are essentially 'private' to the application itself (no user-specific ownership is detailed), a single collection is sufficient, secured at the collection level using basic Firebase Authentication. Because there is no user level roles required for this application, all business logic must be performed in a trusted environment. 

Authorization Independence is achieved by avoiding hierarchical authorization dependencies. There are no subcollections or parent-child relationships that would require `get()` calls in security rules. All required information is self-contained within the `budgets` document.

This structure supports QAPs (Rules Are Not Filters) because the rules primarily focus on whether the user is authenticated to access the collection and do not need to filter based on document content during `list` operations. Filtering (for example, by status) would be handled in the application logic after the data is retrieved, or using composite indexes for server side filtering and pagination.
